bargmann-1991-science.label: Bargmann & Horvitz, Science 1991
bargmann-1991-science.synopsis: Uses laser ablation of identified neurons to map the C. elegans chemosensory system. AWC and AWA neurons detect volatile odorants and mediate olfactory attraction; ASE and ASG detect dissolved ions and drive gustatory responses; ASH responds to noxious chemicals and drives avoidance. Foundational map of chemosensory neuron identities and functions.

bargmann-2006-wormbook.label: Bargmann, WormBook 2006 — Chemosensation
bargmann-2006-wormbook.synopsis: Comprehensive review of C. elegans chemosensation: neuron identities, chemoreceptor gene families, signal transduction via cGMP and calcium channels, and the circuit logic governing approach and avoidance across olfactory and gustatory modalities.

bargmann-2006-wormbook-loco.label: Bargmann, WormBook 2006 — Locomotion
bargmann-2006-wormbook-loco.synopsis: TODO — verify author and chapter title for this WormBook DOI. Currently cited for AVB/AVA command interneuron circuit controlling forward and reverse locomotion.

bargmann-1993-cell.label: Bargmann et al., Cell 1993
bargmann-1993-cell.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Characterizes odorant-selective receptor genes expressed in AWC and AWA neurons; different odorant classes are detected by different neuron subsets, suggesting combinatorial coding of odor identity.

chalfie-1985-j-neurosci.label: Chalfie et al., J Neurosci 1985
chalfie-1985-j-neurosci.synopsis: Defines the mechanosensory touch circuit in C. elegans: ALM and AVM mediate anterior body touch; PLM and PVM mediate posterior touch. Identifies the MEC gene family encoding the mechanosensory ion channel complex (MEC-4/MEC-2) and traces the pathway from touch neurons through interneurons to motor output.

white-1986-phil-trans.label: White et al., Phil Trans R Soc B 1986
white-1986-phil-trans.synopsis: The complete electron-microscopic reconstruction of all 302 neurons and approximately 7,000 synaptic connections in C. elegans — the foundational connectome. Remains the structural reference for all circuit-level interpretation in the field, and establishes C. elegans as a model for whole-brain wiring-level analysis.

kaplan-1993-pnas.label: Kaplan & Horvitz, PNAS 1993
kaplan-1993-pnas.synopsis: Characterizes ASH as a polymodal nociceptor that responds to both harsh mechanical stimuli and aversive chemicals — a single neuron integrating multiple threat signals. ASH synapses onto AVA command interneurons to drive rapid reversals, establishing the cellular basis of nociceptive avoidance in C. elegans.

torayama-2007-j-neurosci.label: Torayama et al., J Neurosci 2007
torayama-2007-j-neurosci.synopsis: Demonstrates positive olfactory associative conditioning in C. elegans: pairing the odorant butanone with bacterial food enhances subsequent chemotaxis to butanone. The conditioning requires AWC^ON neuron activity and is modulated by insulin/IGF-1 signaling, revealing a cellular and molecular basis for experience-dependent odor preference.

chalasani-2010-plos-biol.label: Chalasani et al., PLoS Biol 2010
chalasani-2010-plos-biol.synopsis: Shows that neuropeptide signals released by interneurons feed back onto AWC olfactory neurons, modifying the dynamics of their odor-evoked calcium responses. Demonstrates that circuit feedback can tune primary sensory representations, with implications for adaptation and gain control in chemosensory processing.
chalasani-2010-plos-biol._note: This paper is currently misattributed as 'Kauffman et al.' in nodes.json smell-familiar choice 1 — it is NOT a food-associative learning paper. Do not use as a citation for positive olfactory conditioning.

song-2013-elife.label: Song et al., eLife 2013
song-2013-elife.synopsis: Demonstrates that recognition of a familiar food activates enhanced pharyngeal pumping through an endocrine serotonin signal from NSM neurons. Familiar food odor triggers NSM to release serotonin into the body cavity, accelerating the feeding rate — linking olfactory memory to a downstream change in food intake.

zhang-2005-nature.label: Zhang et al., Nature 2005
zhang-2005-nature.synopsis: Landmark paper demonstrating pathogen-conditioned olfactory aversion in C. elegans. Worms that survive exposure to Pseudomonas aeruginosa subsequently avoid the smell of that pathogen. The aversive memory requires serotonin signaling and persists for hours, establishing C. elegans as a model for experience-dependent behavioral immunity.

ha-2010-neuron.label: Ha et al., Neuron 2010
ha-2010-neuron.synopsis: Maps the neural circuit for aversive olfactory learning in C. elegans, placing AWC olfactory neurons upstream of AIY, AIB, and RIA interneurons. Serotonergic modulation from ADF neurons is required for the memory circuit to form conditioned avoidance, revealing how neuromodulator tone gates learning.

sawin-2000-neuron.label: Sawin et al., Neuron 2000
sawin-2000-neuron.synopsis: Demonstrates that dopamine and serotonin differentially modulate locomotion based on feeding state: food contact slows locomotion rate via dopamine from CEP/ADE mechanosensory neurons, while recent feeding history promotes pharyngeal pumping and adjusts locomotion via serotonin from NSM neurons. Establishes the monoaminergic basis of food-state-dependent behavior.

hills-2004-j-neurosci.label: Hills et al., J Neurosci 2004
hills-2004-j-neurosci.synopsis: Shows that dopamine and glutamate co-regulate area-restricted search in C. elegans. On food, worms suppress reversals and run forward; off food, they increase pirouetting to search locally. Dopamine signals food presence and suppresses local search; its loss leads to inappropriate long-range dispersal.

gray-2005-pnas.label: Gray et al., PNAS 2005
gray-2005-pnas.synopsis: Characterizes the circuit logic of chemotaxis and navigation in C. elegans. Worms modulate reversal and turn frequency based on odor gradient direction, using the pirouette mechanism: reversals are triggered when moving down an attractant gradient, and suppressed when moving up. AIY and AIB interneurons act as sign-inverting and sign-preserving relays in this computation.

worthy-2018-plos-one.label: Worthy et al., PLOS ONE 2018
worthy-2018-plos-one.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Characterizes volatile odorants produced by preferred bacterial food strains and their role in guiding C. elegans food choice via olfaction.

zhang-2016-jbc.label: Zhang et al., J Biol Chem 2016
zhang-2016-jbc.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Describes a 'Trojan Horse' mechanism by which harmful bacteria produce attractive odorants that lure C. elegans, causing olfactory deception that leads to ingestion of a harmful food source.

schiffer-2021-plos-pathogens.label: Schiffer et al., PLOS Pathogens 2021
schiffer-2021-plos-pathogens.synopsis: Demonstrates that C. elegans uses hydrogen peroxide — a bacterially-produced chemical — as a sensory signal to identify environmental niches offering both food and protection from oxidative stress. Shows that chemosensory evaluation extends beyond food location to include environmental safety assessment.

avery-1989-neuron.label: Avery & Horvitz, Neuron 1989
avery-1989-neuron.synopsis: Identifies the pharyngeal nervous system of C. elegans as a semi-autonomous neuromuscular unit with its own pattern generator. The MC neuron excites pharyngeal muscles to drive pump cycles; M3 provides inhibitory input controlling relaxation between cycles. Establishes the basic circuit architecture of feeding-rate control.

avery-2003-j-exp-biol.label: Avery & Shtonda, J Exp Biol 2003
avery-2003-j-exp-biol.synopsis: Demonstrates that C. elegans actively selects among bacterial food sources of different nutritional quality and discriminates against bacteria it cannot efficiently grind. The pm5 grinder muscle is required to lyse bacteria before intestinal absorption — bacteria that cannot be lysed pass through without nutritional benefit, and worms learn to avoid them.

maguire-2011-curr-biol.label: Maguire et al., Curr Biol 2011
maguire-2011-curr-biol.synopsis: Shows that C. elegans can escape from predacious nematophagous fungi by triggering mechanosensory reversal responses on contact with fungal hyphae. The touch circuit provides a last-resort escape mechanism, though constricting ring closure (~100ms) means escape is not guaranteed.

hsueh-2017-elife.label: Hsueh et al., eLife 2017
hsueh-2017-elife.synopsis: Demonstrates that the nematophagous fungus Arthrobotrys oligospora attracts C. elegans by mimicking both food odorants and C. elegans sex pheromones. This chemical mimicry exploits the worm's own chemosensory system to lure prey within range of fungal constricting rings — a fatal attraction.

yu-2021-nat-commun.label: Yu et al., Nat Commun 2021
yu-2021-nat-commun.synopsis: Identifies 6-methyl-salicylic acid (6-MSA) as the volatile molecule produced by A. oligospora responsible for fatal attraction in C. elegans. Provides a specific molecular mechanism for olfactory predator mimicry, showing the worm's olfactory system cannot distinguish this fungal volatile from food-associated odorants.

lin-2025-isci.label: Lin et al., iScience 2025
lin-2025-isci.synopsis: Finds that contact with nematode-trapping fungal hyphae triggers mechanosensory-dependent behavioral quiescence in C. elegans. Characterizes this as a novel defensive or conserved injury-response behavior activated through the touch circuit upon physical contact with fungal structures.

zaslaver-2015-pnas.label: Zaslaver et al., PNAS 2015
zaslaver-2015-pnas.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Examines sparse coding in the C. elegans olfactory system; characterizes how odor identity is represented and compressed across layers of the sensory circuit.

meisel-2014-cell.label: Meisel et al., Cell 2014
meisel-2014-cell.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Characterizes chemosensory detection of bacterially-produced secondary metabolites by C. elegans and the behavioral consequences.

moore-2019a-cell.label: Moore et al., Cell 2019
moore-2019a-cell.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Reports transgenerational epigenetic memory in C. elegans.

moore-2019b-cell.label: Moore et al., Cell 2019
moore-2019b-cell.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Characterizes pathogen avoidance learning and its transmission.

kaletsky-2020-nature.label: Kaletsky et al., Nature 2020
kaletsky-2020-nature.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Demonstrates transgenerational inheritance of pathogen-specific small RNAs that confer avoidance behavior in offspring.

melo-2012-cell.label: Melo & Ruvkun, Cell 2012
melo-2012-cell.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Characterizes how C. elegans activates innate immune-like surveillance pathways in response to bacterial infection.

sternson-2013-curr-opin-neurobiol.label: Sternson et al., Curr Opin Neurobiol 2013
sternson-2013-curr-opin-neurobiol.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Review of neural circuits for hunger motivation across animals; contextualizes C. elegans feeding circuits within broader principles of hunger-driven behavior.

rengarajan-2019-pnas.label: Rengarajan et al., PNAS 2019
rengarajan-2019-pnas.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Feeding state reconfigures sensory valence in C. elegans — the same odor can drive attraction or aversion depending on hunger state, demonstrating that motivational state rewires sensory circuit outputs.

jang-2019-pnas.label: Jang et al., PNAS 2019
jang-2019-pnas.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Multiple sensory neurons mediate starvation-dependent aversive navigation; food deprivation reconfigures the sensory circuit so the same stimulus produces different behavioral outputs.

ryu-2018-embo-j.label: Ryu et al., EMBO J 2018
ryu-2018-embo-j.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Pheromone-mediated avoidance is regulated by insulin signaling, connecting metabolic state to chemosensory valence and revealing how internal nutritional state modulates social chemical signaling.

matty-2022-plos-genetics.label: Matty et al., PLOS Genetics 2022
matty-2022-plos-genetics.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Intestine-to-neuronal intertissue signaling during food deprivation alters risk-taking behavior in C. elegans; gut-derived signals shift the explore/exploit balance toward riskier food-seeking strategies.

chao-2004-nat-neurosci.label: Chao et al., Nat Neurosci 2004
chao-2004-nat-neurosci.synopsis: TODO — confirm synopsis. Serotonin modulates the threshold of ASH-mediated nociceptive avoidance in C. elegans, showing how neuromodulator tone adjusts the sensitivity of aversive sensory responses based on internal state.
